title: Air India's One-Brand Network Reshapes India's Long-Haul Map
dek: A year after absorbing Vistara, the Tata-owned carrier is running a single brand and steadily widening its non-stop reach from Delhi, Mumbai and Bengaluru to Europe and North America.
body:
By late 2025, Air India was operating as one consolidated full-service airline following the completion of its merger with Vistara in November 2024, and the carrier has spent the months since rebuilding a long-haul network that was fragmented for much of the past decade. The integration brought a larger combined fleet, overlapping route authorities and a wider domestic feed into Delhi and Mumbai, giving the Tata Group carrier more depth to compete with Gulf and European rivals on connecting traffic to and from the subcontinent.

A wider non-stop footprint

Air India's strategy has centred on adding or thickening point-to-point services between major Indian metros and demand hubs in Europe and North America, areas where Indian-originating traffic has historically leaked to one-stop itineraries via Dubai, Doha and Abu Dhabi. Bengaluru in particular has emerged as a growing long-haul origin alongside the established Delhi and Mumbai gateways.

The carrier has also leaned on its widebody mix of Boeing 777s, 787 Dreamliners and incoming new-generation aircraft to balance frequency and capacity, while a phased cabin retrofit programme aims to bring older interiors closer to the standard expected on premium intercontinental routes.

Competing for the connecting passenger

The wider goal is to capture more of the traffic that flows between Europe, North America and South and Southeast Asia over an Indian hub rather than a Gulf one. Success depends on schedule reliability, premium-cabin quality and the pace of fleet renewal, all of which remain works in progress.

  • Single brand and frequent-flyer programme across the former Vistara network
  • Delhi and Mumbai as primary long-haul hubs, with Bengaluru growing
  • Widebody retrofits intended to lift premium-cabin consistency

For travellers, the practical effect is a broader menu of Indian-flag non-stops and clearer single-airline connections, though the transition's full benefits will take time to settle across schedules and service.
heroImageAlt: An Air India widebody jet climbing after departure against a clear sky on a long-haul route
